BioSci700 Lab 2: Molecular Clock and Bayesian Phylodynamics

Phylogenetic analysis of Dengue virus serotype 4 (DENV-4)

Your Name (UPI)

2026-03-31

Lab Introduction and Objectives

Welcome to Lab 2 on molecular clock analysis and Bayesian phylodynamics. Building on the alignment and tree-building skills from Lab 1, you will now work with time-stamped viral sequences to assess temporal signal, calibrate a molecular clock, and reconstruct past population dynamics using Bayesian inference. Each section combines guided software tasks with interpretive questions that ask you to connect statistical outputs to biological meaning.

Total marks: 25. Marks are allocated as follows: Section 1 (7 pts), Section 2 (5 pts), Section 3 (12 pts), and 1 pt for overall presentation quality and writing clarity. An additional 1 bonus mark is available in Question 3.8.

Please read the following submission instructions carefully before starting:

  1. File naming: Name your report file using your name and student ID: Lab2_YourName_UPI. Submit as either a PDF or HTML file.
  2. Editor: You may use any editor (e.g. Word, Google Docs, or this .Rmd file) to complete the report. Copy the structure of this document and answer all questions. Insert all required figures to support your results.
  3. Questions: Answer all questions in the provided text blocks marked with the following. Each answer must be no more than 100 words.
Your answer here
  1. Zip: Submit all files related to this assignment — in particular, log files and tree files — for marking purposes. The recommended practice is to keep all files in a single working directory and compress it into a zip file for submission.

Learning Objectives

By the end of this lab, you will be able to:

  1. Understand rate heterogeneity among sites and among lineages
  2. Understand rate-time non-identifiability
  3. Build a maximum likelihood tree using IQ-TREE3 and interpret the best-fit substitution model
  4. Assess temporal signal in a dated phylogeny using root-to-tip regression in TempEst
  5. Configure and run a Bayesian coalescent analysis in BEAST 2 using the Coalescent Bayesian Skyline model
  6. Diagnose MCMC convergence using Tracer and interpret posterior parameter estimates
  7. Reconstruct and interpret past population dynamics from a Bayesian Skyline plot

Software and Data

Ensure the following software is installed before beginning:

  • IQ-TREE3 >3.x — maximum likelihood tree inference
  • TempEst — temporal signal assessment
  • BEAST >2.7.7 — Bayesian evolutionary analysis; includes BEAUti 2 (XML configuration) and TreeAnnotator 2 (tree summarisation)
  • CCD package — install via the BEAST 2 Package Manager
  • Tracer >1.7.x — MCMC diagnostics and skyline reconstruction
  • FigTree or IcyTree — tree visualisation
  • TempEst, Tracer, and FigTree require Java 1.8 or higher
  • Beagle (optional) — hardware-accelerated likelihood computation for BEAST
Orthoflavivirus — image from ViralZone

Dengue virus (DENV) is a positive-sense, single-stranded RNA virus transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes. It comprises four serotypes (DENV-1 to DENV-4) and causes dengue fever, a major public health burden affecting hundreds of millions of people annually across tropical and subtropical regions.

The dataset for this lab is the same alignment used in Bennett et al. (2010): 76 DENV-4 sequences sampled between 1981 and 1998 (Dengue4.nex). Read the publication before beginning; it provides essential biological context for interpreting your results.

Section 1: Maximum Likelihood Tree (7 points)

Build a maximum likelihood (ML) tree from the DENV-4 alignment using IQ-TREE3. Run ModelFinder together with tree reconstruction so that the substitution model is selected automatically.

# Example IQ-TREE3 command (run in terminal)
iqtree3 -s Dengure4.nex -m MFP

IQ-TREE3 evaluated many candidate substitution models and selected one as the best fit for this dataset. The selection is performed by ModelFinder, which scores each candidate model using an information criterion that balances goodness-of-fit against model complexity. Understanding the selected model — including how it handles substitution rates and among-site rate variation — is essential for interpreting the resulting tree and for configuring a consistent model in downstream Bayesian analyses.

Question 1.1 (2 pts)

State the best-fit model reported in the IQ-TREE3 log and also name the criterion used to select the model. Read the IQ-TREE3 documentation and explain what each component of the model name represents.

Your answer here

Question 1.2 (2 pts)

According to the best-fit model selected by IQ-TREE3, identify which part of the model handles rate heterogeneity among sites. Briefly describe how this site model is an improvement over a traditional alternative (name the alternative model as well).

Your answer here

Question 1.3 (1 pt)

Visualise the maximum likelihood tree (e.g. Dengure4.treefile) using FigTree or IcyTree, or ggtree.

Insert your tree figure below.

Question 1.4 (2 pts)

Using this maximum likelihood tree as an example, briefly explain the rate-time non-identifiability problem and why it prevents a direct interpretation of branch lengths as evolutionary time.

Files required, such as: Dengure4.treefile, Dengure4.log, Dengure4.iqtree

Section 2: Temporal Signal (4 points)

We are going to use TempEst to explore the temporal structure of heterochronous sequences in this section.

Open the ML tree in TempEst 1.5. Parse tip dates by clicking “Parse Dates”, setting Order to last and Prefix to DNF. Enable “Add the following value to each:” and click OK. Switch to the “Tree” tab to inspect the tree.

Click image to zoom in

Next, enable “best-fitting root” in the top-left panel to re-root the tree at the position that maximises the molecular clock signal using the heuristic residual mean squared method.

Click image to zoom in

TempEst is a tool for exploring the temporal signal of molecular phylogenies. It can re-root a tree to optimise clock-like behaviour, assess temporal structure by regressing root-to-tip divergence against sampling time, and identify outlier sequences that violate the molecular clock assumption — all of which are essential checks before committing to a time-calibrated Bayesian analysis.

Question 2.1 (1 pt)

Before enabling the best-fitting root, record the statistics from the root-to-tip regression panel for the original ML root in the table below. Then enable the best-fitting root and record the updated statistics.

Insert a screenshot of the root-to-tip regression plot after selecting the best-fitting root below.

Record all four statistics for both rootings in the table below.

Question 2.2 (2 pts)

Interpret the implications of these four statistics. Conclude whether this dataset favours the strict molecular clock or relaxed clock model, and why.

Insert screenshots of the tree displayed in the “Tree” tab for both the original ML root and the best-fitting root, ensuring tip labels are visible. Briefly describe the key structural difference between these two trees.

Your answer here

Section 3: BEAST 2 Phylodynamic Analysis (12 points)

In this section you will use BEAST 2 to perform a Bayesian phylodynamic analysis of time-stamped DENV-4 sequences. You will work with three key features of BEAST 2:

  1. Codon partitioning — the alignment is split into codon positions and non-coding positions, each assigned a relative substitution rate, allowing the model to account for rate variation across different functional classes of sites.
  2. Tip date calibration — sampling dates are provided for each sequence, enabling BEAST 2 to estimate the molecular clock rate directly from the temporal signal in the data.
  3. Coalescent Bayesian Skyline (Drummond et al., 2005) — a flexible non-parametric tree prior that reconstructs changes in effective population size through time without assuming a fixed demographic model.

0.1 Setting up the model in BEAUti

Follow these steps to configure the BEAST XML file:

  1. Partitions — Import Dengue4.nex via File > Import Alignment. The nexus file includes pre-defined codon partitions, so BEAUti will automatically display four partitions (codon positions 1, 2, 3, and a non-coding partition), each with 76 taxa. Click Link Clock Models and Link Trees to share the same clock model and tree topology across all partitions, while keeping site models unlinked so each partition estimates its own substitution rate.

  2. Tip Dates — Check “Use tip dates”. Keep the default Since some time in the past, which is known as forward time in a phylodynamic analysis. Click “Auto-config”, choose “after first”, replace _ with F, and select “Add fixed value to 1900”. Click OK and verify the parsed years in the table.

  3. Site Model — Select HKY with 4 Gamma rate categories. Estimate the substitution rate, gamma shape, kappa, and base frequencies. You may use the Clone function to replicate the configuration: first configure one site model, then hold shift and click the remaining site models on the left to select all, and click OK to clone the settings from the configured model to the others. Verify that all site models now show the same configuration.

  4. Clock Model — Keep Strict Clock as the molecular clock model, and set the starting clock rate to 9.0E-4.

  5. Priors — Set the tree prior to Coalescent Bayesian Skyline, and change the starting value of Pop Sizes to 50. Change the clockRate prior from Uniform to Log Normal with M = -7.15 and S = 0.25. You can verify the prior quantiles using:

qlnorm(p = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975), meanlog = -7.15, sdlog = 0.25)

  1. Initialization — Show the initialization panel (View > Show initialization panel). Expand both bPopSizes and bGroupSizes to verify that their dimensions match the number of Skyline intervals (default: 5).

  2. MCMC — Set chain length to 25,000,000. Log every 5,000 for both the trace log and tree log. Log every 100,000 for the screen log. Save as Dengue4.xml.

Question 3.1 (2 pts)

In BEAUti, the default prior for clockRate is Uniform(0, ∞). You have replaced this with a LogNormal(M = −7.15, S = 0.25) prior. Think about what a Bayesian prior represents biologically, and answer the questions below:

  • Why is a Uniform prior problematic for a rate parameter like clockRate?
  • Use the qlnorm() output from the setup to describe what the LogNormal prior implies biologically, namely, what range of clock rates does it consider plausible for DENV-4?
  • A Normal distribution is also informative. Why is it nonetheless unsuitable for a rate parameter, and what property of the LogNormal makes it more appropriate?
Your answer here

0.2 Running and diagnosing the BEAST analysis

Run Dengue4.xml using BEAST 2 (either launch the BEAST app and select the file, or run beast Dengue4.xml from the terminal), saving all output files to your working directory. Once the run is complete, open the trace log (Dengue4.log) in Tracer. Set a burn-in of 10% and inspect the ESS values and trace plots for all parameters to assess whether the MCMC chain has converged.

Question 3.2 (2 pts)

  • Insert a screenshot of the Tracer ESS summary. Report the posterior mean and 95% HPD for the clock rate and tree height. Did all parameters meet the minimum ESS threshold commonly used in Bayesian phylogenetics? If not, which did not, and what would you do to address this?
  • You ran a single BEAST chain. What would be the advantage of running a second independent chain, and how would you use it to verify that both chains converged to the same posterior distribution?
Your answer here

0.3 The relative rates

In Tracer, find the four relative rates (prefixed “mutationRate”) in the parameter list. Hold Ctrl (Windows/Linux) or Cmd (Mac) and click each one to select all four, then switch to the “Marginal Density” tab to view their marginal posterior probability densities.

Question 3.3 (2 pts)

  • Insert a screenshot of the marginal densities.
  • Compute the absolute rate of the 1st codon position and show your calculation.
  • Considering the biological meaning of each partition, draw a conclusion about codon-specific rate heterogeneity in DENV-4 based on your results.
Your answer here

0.4 Summarising trees

To summarise the trees logged during MCMC, we will use the CCD methods implemented in the program TreeAnnotator to create the maximum a posteriori (MAP) tree. You need to install the CCD package to make the options available in TreeAnnotator. Set Dengue4.trees as the input file, set 10% as the burn-in percentage, and select “MAP (CCD0)” as the “Target tree type”. After it completes, visualise the MAP tree in FigTree or IcyTree.

Question 3.4 (2 pts)

  • What are the units of branch lengths in the MAP tree?
  • Report the estimated age of the most recent common ancestor (tMRCA) of all samples and its 95% HPD interval.
  • Interpret this estimate in the context of the DENV-4 epidemic history.
Your answer here

0.5 Bayesian Skyline reconstruction

In Tracer, navigate to Analysis > Bayesian Skyline Reconstruction. Set the “Age of youngest tip” to 1998, the year of the most recently sampled sequence in this dataset. Click OK to generate the skyline plot.

Question 3.5 (1 pt)

What is the biological meaning of effective population size in this viral analysis?

Your answer here

Question 3.6 (1 pt)

Insert your Bayesian Skyline plot from the default analysis (dimension = 5). Describe any population dynamics you can observe: did the effective population size (Ne) increase, decrease, or remain stable over the sampling period?

Your answer here

Question 3.7 (1 pt)

Compare your Bayesian Skyline plot with Fig. 3 in Bennett et al. (2010) in terms of the reconstructed Ne trajectory, temporal resolution, and credible interval width. Which result is more informative, and what specific change to the model setting could bring the less informative result closer to the better one?

Your answer here

Question 3.8 (2 pts + 1 bonus)

Write a concise methods-and-results summary of your BEAST 2 analysis, in the style of a brief research summary. Your summary should cover:

  • The models applied and the rationale for key choices (e.g., substitution model, clock model, tree prior)
  • The interpretation of key estimated parameters (e.g., clock rate, tMRCA) in the context of DENV-4 biology
  • What the Bayesian Skyline plot reveals about DENV-4 population dynamics between 1981 and 1998

Bonus: Identify one limitation of your analysis and propose a concrete improvement.

Your answer here
